facts_disclosed: Planning second shop in Moda with Burak managing

turns.content: i want to open a second location for my business but i'm not sure if it's the right time. can you help me think it through?

Absolutely, I'd be happy to help you think through an expansion. Let's start with the basics: what kind of business do you run, where is the current location, and where are you thinking for the second one? And what's making you consider it now?

I am a barber... traditional barber in Kadıköy, Istanbul. 30 years I have been here. and I am thinking... Moda. you know Moda? it is close to Kadıköy but different... younger crowd, more cafes, artists, good energy. I have been thinking about this for a long time now.

I know Moda — it's a great neighbourhood. Very walkable, lots of foot traffic, and the demographic skews younger and more affluent. For a traditional barber, that could actually be a strong draw because there's a real trend toward artisanal, old-school grooming among younger men.

But a second location is a big step. A few key questions:
- Who would run the Moda shop day-to-day? Would you split your time, or do you have someone who could manage it?
- Do you have a sense of what rent would be in Moda?
- How would you fund the setup costs?

this is the thing... I have Burak. my apprentice. he has been with me three years now and he is... he is almost ready. his hands are good, his technique is clean, the customers like him. I have been training him the way Hasan usta trained me — the old way, proper. and I think... I think he could manage the Moda shop.

Having a trained apprentice you trust is probably the single biggest factor that makes a second location viable. If you had to hire a stranger, I'd be much more cautious. But someone you've trained yourself for three years, who knows your standards and your methods — that's different.

How confident are you in Burak's ability to handle the business side? Running a shop isn't just about cutting hair — it's dealing with suppliers, managing the schedule, keeping the books, handling difficult customers. Has he been exposed to any of that?

some of it... he sees how I run things, he knows the suppliers, he orders the pomade and the razors already. the books... no, Ayşe does the books for me, hah. she does the books and the alterations both, she is a seamstress you know, works from our flat above the shop. maybe she teaches Burak the numbers too. but you are right, managing is different from cutting. I would need to be there at the beginning... maybe split my time for the first months.

That's a realistic plan. Most successful second locations work exactly this way — the owner spends the first 3-6 months heavily present at the new shop to set the standard, train on the operational side, and build the initial customer base. Then you gradually step back as the manager finds their feet.

The fact that Burak already handles ordering is a good sign. For the bookkeeping, you could either have Ayşe manage both shops' books centrally, or invest in some basic training for Burak. Keeping it with Ayşe is probably simpler at first.

Now, let's talk about the money. What are rents like in Moda for a small shop space?

I have been looking... talking to people. a small space, good location on one of the side streets near the coast... maybe 10,000 lira a month. it is more than Kadıköy — I pay 8,500 in Kadıköy — but the foot traffic in Moda... everyone walks there. on the weekends it is full of people. I think it could work.

10,000 in Moda versus 8,500 in Kadıköy — that's a 1,500 lira premium, but you're right that Moda's foot traffic is exceptional, especially on weekends. The walk-in potential alone could justify the difference.

Let me help you think about the startup costs beyond rent. For a new barber shop, you'd need:
- **First month's rent + deposit** — probably 20,000-30,000 upfront
- **Chairs, mirrors, stations** — are you buying new or can you source secondhand?
- **Renovation/fitting out** — plumbing for a wash basin, good lighting, flooring
- **Initial supplies** — towels, products, tools
- **Signage**

Do you have a sense of how much you've saved, or what budget you're working with for the setup?

I have been saving... for two years now, quietly. Ayşe knows of course, Ayşe knows everything, hah. we have maybe 80,000 lira put aside. and my friend Osman, he closed his shop last year — retired — he has two chairs and mirrors he would sell me cheap. so the big costs are the renovation and the deposit.

80,000 saved plus cheap secondhand equipment from Osman — that's a solid starting position. Let me rough out the numbers:

- Deposit + first month: ~30,000
- Osman's chairs and mirrors: let's say 10,000-15,000 if he's giving a friend price
- Renovation (plumbing, paint, flooring, lighting): 20,000-35,000 depending on how much work the space needs
- Initial supplies and tools for Burak's station: 5,000-8,000
- Signage and small details: 3,000-5,000

That puts you at roughly 70,000-90,000 for the full setup. Your 80,000 covers most of it, but it's tight. The risk is having no cash buffer if something unexpected comes up in the first few months — and something always does.

Would you consider keeping 20,000-30,000 in reserve and financing a portion of the setup? Or do you want to avoid any debt?

no debt... no. I have never borrowed for the business and I will not start now. in Turkish we say 'borç yiğidin kamçısıdır' — debt is the whip of a brave man. no, I will wait until I have enough saved with a cushion. maybe two, three more months of saving. and Burak needs those months too... to learn more of the business side like you said.

That's a wise approach — and those two or three months serve double duty. You build up the financial cushion and Burak builds up his management skills. Use that time intentionally:

- Start having Burak close up the shop on his own
- Let him handle a full day solo while you observe from a distance
- Have Ayşe walk him through the basic bookkeeping
- Take him to Moda to look at spaces together so he feels ownership of the plan

By the time you open, he won't just be your apprentice managing a shop — he'll be a partner in the expansion who helped build it from the idea stage. That kind of investment in someone pays back enormously.

you know... you say 'partner' and it makes me think. Burak is like... he is not my son but he is the closest thing I have to passing on what I know. Hasan usta gave everything to me, all his knowledge, all his wisdom. I must give it to someone too. that is how the craft survives... from hand to hand, generation to generation. this shop in Moda... it is not just business. it is the continuation.

That's a beautiful way to see it — and honestly, that kind of purpose behind a business decision is what separates the ones that last from the ones that don't. You're not just opening a second location for revenue. You're extending a lineage that goes back to Hasan usta and probably before him.

Burak is lucky to have a master who sees it that way. And Moda will be lucky to have a shop that carries that tradition.

It sounds like you have a clear plan: save for two or three more months, train Burak on operations, source the equipment from Osman, and look at spaces. When you're ready, you'll know.

yes... I will know. the way Hasan usta always said, 'acele işe şeytan karışır' — the devil interferes with rushed work. no rush. we do it right. thank you my friend, you have helped me see it more clearly. I will talk to Burak tomorrow... carefully. I do not want to scare him, hah. güle güle.
Güle güle, Mehmet. Don't scare the boy — just plant the seed and let it grow. He'll be ready when the time comes. Good luck with the saving and the planning. Moda won't know what hit it.
